those quotes, I made a point of not
imitating the accents or the
intonations, and I gave you no visual
clues whatsoever to help you out. They
came from a wide range of different
movies from different genres, different
decades and styles. So, there's no
common thread to pick up on that might
help you out. But does any of that
matter? Nope. Because I'll bet that
pretty much every one of you could name
not just the movie they came from, but
also the actors who said them and
probably picture the exact scene in your
heads. Let's try another one, shall we?
Luke Skywalker, Captain Kirk, James
Bond, Sarah Connor, Sherlock Holmes,
Rocky Baloa, Bruce Wayne, Marty McFly,
Indiana Jones, Forest Gump, Hannibal
Lectar. The moment I say those names, it
instantly conjures up an image of that
person in your mind, the actor that
played them, the most iconic moments
they took part in, and even the emotions
that the character inspires. That right
there, my friends, is the power of the
cultural footprint. It's the ability of
films to transcend the medium they
started in and become a living,
breathing part of our culture,
referenced and quoted and thought about
by generation after generation. Here's
the problem, though. Not a single one of
them was created in the past 20 years.
[ __ ] Yeah, that's kind of worrying,
isn't it? And before you start accusing
me of cherry-picking my examples just to
prove a point, I can tell you that I
looked up six different lists of the
hundred best movie quotes of all time
for this video, read every single one
from start to finish, and not a single
one belong to a movie made after 2005.
Look up any list of iconic movie
characters, and I can pretty much
guarantee they'll all be from 2010 or
earlier. And even if they do exist in
newer movies like the Marvel or DC
universes, the characters themselves
will have been created decades earlier
in books, comics, or TV shows. What
you're seeing today is just another
iteration of something that was invented
generations ago. Just another meal made
by someone else reheated in the cultural
microwave. One of the saddest emails I
ever read was from a young subscriber of
mine complaining that their generation
doesn't really have anything to call
their own. no unique cultural touchstone
they can point to and say, "See that?
That was ours. That was our Star Wars,
our Lord of the Rings, our rock and
roll." Everything they see and watch and
listen to now is either a continuation
of some old franchise or a remake of
something that came out decades ago. And
all of this got me thinking, "Wow, we're
living in a bit of a cultural wasteland
these days, aren't we? We're not really
creating much of anything new or
memorable anymore. We're just rehashing
stuff that's already been done before,
making copies of copies that gradually
degenerate into mediocrity and failure.
I mean, just take a look at the three
big movies in theaters this month.
Jurassic World, the seventh movie in the
Jurassic Park franchise. Fantastic 4,
the third movie version of these
characters in the past 20 years, based
on a comic from 60 years ago. and
Superman, the third iteration in the
past 20 years, not counting TV shows and
video games, of course, based on a comic
from 80 years ago. I mean, don't get me
wrong, they make money, that's for sure.
Well, maybe not Fantastic 4, but you get
my point. Jurassic World's on track to
make a billion dollars, and Superman
will probably do okay as well. Although,
even that's looking a bit shaky right
now. But box office revenue isn't the
same as Cultural Impact, and that is the
big difference here. This time next
year, in fact, [ __ ] it. Probably this
time next month, nobody's even going to
remember a single scene, character, or
line of dialogue from Jurassic World:
Afterbirth. [ __ ] man. I can barely even
remember it now. Nobody's going to be
quoting or referencing James Gun
Superman by James Gun by the time it
leaves theaters. They're the movie
equivalent of a fast food meal,
something to be consumed quickly and
then forgotten about just as quickly. So
that if it wasn't for the heartburn and
the explosive diarrhea later in the day,
you probably wouldn't be sure if you'd
even eaten it. The Avatar movies are
probably the peak example of all this.
Each of them makes more money than a
small African country, and the next one
will be no different. But nobody ever
talks about them. Nobody posts fan
theories or recites their most famous
lines or references iconic characters
and other works because, well, there's
nothing to reference. They're nothing
but hollow spectacle devoid of deeper
meaning or substance. As pointless and
empty as that hotel in North Korea with
all the lights on the outside that's
been sat unoccupied for 30 years. Even
Avengers Endgame, arguably the single
biggest movie event of all time, is
nothing more than a footnote in film
history at this point. Nobody talks
about it anymore, except maybe to
reference it as the turning point where
the MCU started its downward plunge into
abject shittiness. I guess part of the
problem there is that we've been wowed
by visual spectacle so many times now
that it creates a kind of numbness in
the audience. You're never really going
to replicate the sheer impact of that
first star destroyer in A New Hope or
the T-Rex attack in Jurassic Park or the
rain soaked neon cityscapes of
Bladeunner because we've seen it all
before now. It might still be
impressive, but it's rarely memorable.
But films are more than just bright
colors on screen. The most memorable
characters and moments in film history
are usually just regular old people in
normal places delivering lines that
actually mean something because of good
writing without half the universe having
to explode in the backgrounds. And we're
just not getting them anymore. And it's
not just films. It feels like every
aspect of modern culture has been
weirdly dulled and toned down and
homogenized. I mean, come on. You must
have noticed it yourself, even if it was
only subconsciously. that weird feeling
of inertia, of stagnation, like we're
not really growing or advancing anymore.
We're just sort of continuing like
butter scraped over too much bread, you
might say. Try this. Pick any decade of
the 20th century, and I reckon most
people could probably identify it based
on the clothes, the technology, the
music, or the films. Each one had its
own unique identity, its own fads and
fashions and zeitgeists. from the rock
and roll of the 50s to the angst and
counterculture of the 70s to the rise of
the internet in the '9s. But at some
point, probably around the late 2000s or
the early 2010s, it all seemed to slow
down and then, well, stop. There were no
definable fashions anymore. Technology
became iterative instead of
revolutionary. Pop music became
homogenized covers of songs from a
decade earlier, and the era of the
remake and the reboot kicked off in
Hollywood big time. We stopped moving
forward and we began looking backwards
and it feels like we've been stuck in
that loop ever since. And quite simply,
that is not good for any of us. So,
where's it all heading? Are we all just
circling the drain of cultural oblivion?
Or are we on the brink of a new golden
age of enlightenment and progress? Well,
for my own sanity and yours, I suspect I
want to believe in the latter. I hope
that what we're seeing now is the tail
end of a cultural era that's been kept
on life support way beyond its natural
time by corporate greed, laziness, and
risk aversion. A lot of the dusty old
franchises that our parents' generation
grew up on and we've been clinging to
like comfort blankets for decades are
reaching the end of their cultural
relevance now, mostly because of
horrendous mismanagement and creative
retardation. But what the [ __ ]
Ultimately, it was going to happen
anyway no matter what. Hollywood's own
incompetence has just sped up the
process. I think we're reaching that
tipping point now. And maybe I was wrong
at the beginning when I described the
modern landscape as a cultural
wasteland. Maybe it's more like a
cultural dam that's been holding back
the flood for too long. The cracks are
starting to appear and sooner or later
it's going to burst. And if that's the
case, it'll be interesting to see what's
left after the floodwaters recedes.
Anyway, that's all I've got for today.
Go away now.
